ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Chilli Garlic Prawn; closest defensible match is stir-fried shrimp dish (FOODON_03315595). Prawns and shrimp are used interchangeably in Singaporean culinary context.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJM covers cooked crustaceans; applied as closest defensible anchor for stir-fried prawn dish with chilli-garlic sauce. No exact FoodEx2 entry for this specific preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific stir-fried prawn dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chilli Garlic Prawn is approximately 250 g, typically served as a shared side dish or individual main course alongside steamed rice in a Singaporean restaurant setting.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shrimp, cooked, stir-fried with vegetables and sauce (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); supplemented by HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database for sauce and condiment components
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chilli Garlic Prawn exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and stir-fried shrimp with sauce, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for comparable stir-fried seafood dishes. Input per_100g values (energy 160 kcal, protein 11.67 g, fat 9.33 g, carbohydrate 8.33 g, sodium 400 mg) are consistent with this adapted composite and are retained with medium confidence. Micronutrient values (calcium 33.33 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 100 mg, saturated fat 1.67 g, trans fat 0.03 g, vitamin C 13.33 mg) are plausible for this preparation and are retained. Fields not reported in the adapted source are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on stir-fried mixed seafood dish with sauce
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chilli Garlic Prawn. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous stir-fried seafood dishes with mixed sauce components from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). The dish is protein-dominant (11.67 g/100g) with moderate carbohydrate (8.33 g/100g) from sauce thickeners and vegetables; protein and fat content attenuate glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (35) × carbohydrate per serving (8.33 g/100g × 250 g / 100) / 100 = 35 × 20.83 / 100 = 7.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100 g — at the upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods, driven by soy sauce, chilli sauce, and tomato paste in the stir-fry sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and chilli sauce to bring sodium below 300 mg/100 g, avoid additional dipping sauces, and limit portion size to no more than 150 g per meal to keep total sodium intake within safe bounds.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (8.33 g/100 g) with low GI (~35) and high protein (11.67 g/100 g) from prawns; cornstarch coating adds refined carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chilli Garlic Prawn has a low estimated glycaemic index and is protein-rich, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served without white rice; request minimal cornstarch coating and pair with non-starchy vegetables to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 100 mg/100 g from prawns and sodium 400 mg/100 g from sauce; saturated fat is low at 1.67 g/100 g and trans fat is negligible at 0.03 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to 150 g or less, request reduced-sodium sauce preparation, and note that while prawn cholesterol is present, the low saturated fat and stir-fried (not deep-fried) preparation make this a more heart-friendly seafood option than battered or fried alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (chilli, tomato paste) may aggravate RA in sensitive individuals; high sodium may worsen tissue swelling; prawns provide anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor personal tolerance to nightshade ingredients (chilli, tomato paste), request reduced-sodium sauce, and note that the lean prawn protein and garlic polyphenols offer anti-inflammatory benefit when the dish is prepared with minimal sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Garlic Prawn is a stir-fried seafood dish popular in Singapore's Indo-Chinese restaurant cuisine, characterised by succulent whole or halved prawns cooked in a bold, aromatic sauce of fresh garlic, red chilli, spring onion, soy sauce, chilli sauce, and tomato paste, thickened lightly with cornstarch. The dish is served hot as a main course or shared side dish, typically accompanied by steamed jasmine rice. The texture is firm and juicy from the prawns, with a glossy, slightly sticky sauce that delivers a balance of heat, savouriness, and mild sweetness. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 160 kcal, 11.67 g protein, 9.33 g fat (saturated fat 1.67 g), 8.33 g carbohydrate, 2.67 g sugar, 1.0 g dietary fibre, and 400 mg sodium. Cholesterol is 100 mg/100 g from the prawns. The estimated glycaemic index is low (~35), making it suitable for blood glucose management when portion-controlled. The high sodium content (400 mg/100 g) is the primary clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Prawns contribute lean protein, some omega-3 fatty acids, and micronutrients including calcium (33.33 mg/100 g), iron (1.0 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (13.33 mg/100 g from chilli and spring onion). The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free but contains crustacean and soy allergens. It is not suitable for those with shellfish allergy.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chilli Garlic Prawn exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp data and HPB Singapore stir-fried seafood composites. Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this adapted source and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable stir-fried seafood dishes. Medium confidence reflects the adapted source match and AI-generated provenance of the input.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, and moisture data for this specific stir-fried chilli garlic prawn preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) or HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition database as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.03 g/100g (trace level), retained from input as consistent with minimal trans fat in stir-fried vegetable oil preparations. All core macros (protein_g 11.67, fat_g 9.33, carbohydrate_g 8.33) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a stir-fried prawn dish. Portions in input contained duplicates (two entries of 300 g and one of 250 g, all marked is_default=true); default serving size set to 250 g as the more conservative and commonly cited restaurant portion. Input cultural tags (Curry Village, Biryani, Butter Chicken) reflect the GrabFood restaurant listing context and are not carried forward as cuisine descriptors. Dish is classified as Indo-Chinese / Singaporean Indian based on preparation style and restaurant context. Halal status assessed as likely based on ingredients; not certified — consumers should verify with the restaurant.
